topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
prompt: Given an integer $n > 1$, let $1 = a_1 < a_2 < \dots < a_t = n - 1$ be all the positive integers that are less than $n$ and coprime to $n$. Determine all values of $n$ for which there is no index $i \in \{1, \dots, t-1\}$ satisfying $3 \mid a_i + a_{i+1}$.
response_text:
Let
\[
R(n)=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_t\}=\{1\le a<n:\gcd(a,n)=1\},
\]
with \(a_1=1,\ a_t=n-1\). We want all \(n>1\) such that for every consecutive pair,
\[
3\nmid a_i+a_{i+1}.
\]

We determine all such \(n\).

---

## 1. Separate by divisibility by \(3\)

We look at residues mod \(3\).

If \(3\nmid n\), then among numbers coprime to \(n\), both residue classes \(1\) and \(2\pmod 3\) occur infinitely often, and a sum is divisible by \(3\) exactly when the two residues are \(1\) and \(2\). So to avoid such a pair among consecutive reduced residues, all consecutive reduced residues would need to have the same residue mod \(3\). That is impossible once there are enough such numbers. We make this precise below.

If \(3\mid n\), then any number coprime to \(n\) is not divisible by \(3\), so every \(a_i\equiv 1\) or \(2\pmod 3\).

A key observation:

- If two consecutive reduced residues have different residues mod \(3\), then their sum is divisible by \(3\).
- Therefore our condition is equivalent to: **all consecutive \(a_i,a_{i+1}\) have the same residue mod \(3\)**.
- Hence all \(a_i\) must have the same residue mod \(3\).

But \(a_1=1\equiv 1\pmod 3\), so all \(a_i\equiv 1\pmod 3\). In particular,
\[
a_t=n-1\equiv 1\pmod 3.
\]
Thus \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\).

So a necessary condition is
\[
n\equiv 2\pmod 3.
\]

---

## 2. Eliminate all \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\) except possibly very small ones

Assume \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\).

Then \(1\in R(n)\), and \(2\in R(n)\) iff \(n\) is odd. If \(n\) is odd and \(n>2\), then \(1,2\) are consecutive reduced residues at the beginning, and
\[
1+2=3,
\]
divisible by \(3\), contradiction.

So for any solution \(n>2\), \(n\) must be even.

Now let \(n\) be even and \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\), so \(n\equiv 2\pmod 6\).

Then \(1\in R(n)\), \(2\notin R(n)\), and \(3\notin R(n)\) (since \(3\nmid n\)? Actually \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\), so \(3\nmid n\), hence \(3\in R(n)\) unless \(n=3\), impossible here). Since \(n\) is even, \(\gcd(2,n)\ne 1\), but \(\gcd(3,n)=1\). Thus the first two reduced residues are
\[
a_1=1,\qquad a_2=3.
\]
Then
\[
a_1+a_2=1+3=4,
\]
not divisible by \(3\), so no contradiction yet.

Consider the next odd numbers coprime to \(n\). Since \(3\nmid n\), both \(3\) and \(5\) are coprime to \(n\) unless \(5\mid n\). If \(5\nmid n\), then \(a_2=3,\ a_3=5\), and
\[
3+5=8\not\equiv 0\pmod 3.
\]
Still no contradiction.

But then \(7\) is coprime unless \(7\mid n\), etc. To avoid a consecutive pair with residues \(1\) and \(2\mod 3\), all reduced residues would need to stay in one residue class mod \(3\). Yet among odd numbers coprime to \(n\), residues mod \(3\) alternate \(1,0,2,1,0,2,\dots\), and excluding multiples of prime divisors of \(n\) cannot remove all numbers from one of the two nonzero residue classes unless \(n\) contains enough primes to kill them all. But \(1\) and \(n-1\equiv 1\pmod3\) are both in class \(1\), while \(3\in R(n)\) gives class \(0\) impossible—wait, since \(3\nmid n\), \(3\) is indeed coprime to \(n\), so \(3\) belongs to the reduced residue system. But \(3\equiv 0\pmod 3\), and then
\[
1+3\equiv 1\pmod3,\qquad 3+5\equiv 2\pmod3.
\]
This shows the previous “all same residue mod \(3\)” argument was too strong because residues \(0\pmod3\) can occur when \(3\nmid n\).

So we handle the cases directly.

---

## 3. Case \(3\nmid n\)

Then \(3\in R(n)\).

### (a) If \(n\) is odd

Then \(2\in R(n)\), so the first reduced residues are \(1,2,\dots\), hence
\[
1+2=3,
\]
bad. So no odd \(n>1\).

### (b) If \(n\) is even

Then \(2\notin R(n)\), and since \(3\in R(n)\), the first two reduced residues are
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=3.
\]
Now if \(5\in R(n)\), then \(a_3=5\), and
\[
3+5=8\not\equiv 2\pmod3.
\]
No contradiction. But if \(7\in R(n)\), then consecutive odd reduced residues among \(3,5,7,\dots\) will eventually include one pair congruent to \(1,2\pmod3\), giving a bad sum. So only very small \(n\) can work.

Check small even \(n\) with \(3\nmid n\):

- \(n=2\): reduced residues \(\{1\}\). No pair, so works.
- \(n=4\): reduced residues \(\{1,3\}\), sum \(4\), works.
- \(n=8\): reduced residues \(\{1,3,5,7\}\); \(1+3=4,\ 3+5=8,\ 5+7=12\), and \(12\) is divisible by \(3\), so fails.
- For any larger even \(n\) with \(3\nmid n\), there are at least four odd reduced residues in increasing order, and among three consecutive gaps of size \(2\), residues mod \(3\) cycle, forcing some adjacent pair to sum to \(0\pmod3\). So no more solutions.

Thus from this case we get \(n=2,4\).

---

## 4. Case \(3\mid n\)

Then every \(a_i\equiv 1\) or \(2\pmod3\). As observed at the start, if any consecutive pair has different residues mod \(3\), their sum is divisible by \(3\). Hence all \(a_i\) must have the same residue mod \(3\).

Since \(a_1=1\equiv1\pmod3\), all \(a_i\equiv1\pmod3\). In particular,
\[
a_t=n-1\equiv1\pmod3,
\]
so \(n\equiv2\pmod3\), contradicting \(3\mid n\).

Therefore no \(n\) divisible by \(3\) works.

---

Hence the only values are
\[
\boxed{2,4}.
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{n=2 \text{ or } n=4}\).
